the-wall: The Wall is a massive fortification of ice, stone, and ancient magic stretching along the northern border of the Seven Kingdoms, manned by the Night's Watch. Built after the Long Night to guard the realms of men against wildlings and the threat beyond, it has stood for thousands of years as one of the largest man-made structures in the known world.
city-kings-landing: King's Landing is the capital of the Seven Kingdoms, founded by Aegon the Conqueror at the site where his fleet landed before the Conquest of Westeros. Dominated by the Red Keep, it is the seat of the Iron Throne and the center of political power in the realm, as well as its largest and most populous city.
castle-hayford-castle: Hayford Castle is the seat of House Hayford, a minor noble house of the crownlands less than a day's ride from King's Landing, sworn directly to the Iron Throne. During the First Blackfyre Rebellion, its lord briefly served as Hand of the King to Daeron II Targaryen, and the house had earlier supported Rhaenyra Targaryen's claim during the Dance of the Dragons.
castle-rosby: Rosby is the seat of House Rosby, a minor noble house of the crownlands just northeast of King's Landing, sworn directly to the Iron Throne. Their blazon is three red chevronels on ermine.
other-old-stone-bridge-inn: The Old Stone Bridge Inn is a wayside inn at a river crossing on the road between Rosby and Duskendale in the crownlands. Brienne of Tarth lodges there with her traveling companions while searching for Sansa Stark, before riding on alone toward Duskendale.
town-duskendale: Duskendale is a port town on Blackwater Bay in the crownlands, seat of House Rykker following the extinction of House Darklyn. It is best remembered for the Defiance of Duskendale, when Lord Denys Darklyn held King Aerys II Targaryen captive within the town for months, an event that destroyed his house and left a lasting mark on the king's sanity.
castle-stokeworth: Stokeworth is the seat of House Stokeworth, a minor noble house of the crownlands sworn directly to King's Landing.
castle-rooks-rest: Rook's Rest is a castle on the northern shore of Blackwater Bay in the crownlands, seat of House Staunton. During the Dance of the Dragons, it was the site of a battle in which Rhaenys Targaryen and her dragon Meleys, arriving to relieve the besieged castle, were killed by the combined attack of Aegon II and Aemond Targaryen on their dragons Sunfyre and Vhagar.
castle-dyre-den: Dyre Den is a small castle of three crooked towers on a cliff above Crackclaw Point in the crownlands, seat of House Brune of the Dyre Den. It is reached only by a steep, narrow cleft cut into the cliff face below the walls.
ruin-the-whispers: The Whispers is a ruined castle of House Crabb on Crackclaw Point in the crownlands, long abandoned and overgrown with forest. Legend holds it takes its name from the whispering, disembodied heads of victims left by the fabled knight Ser Clarence Crabb, and in the novels it serves as a smugglers' cove where Brienne of Tarth confronts members of the Brave Companions.
castle-dragonstone: Dragonstone is an island castle in Blackwater Bay, shaped by the Valyrians in the likeness of dragons and settled by House Targaryen more than a century before the Doom of Valyria. It served as Aegon the Conqueror's staging ground for the conquest of Westeros and, afterward, as the traditional seat of the heir to the Iron Throne, the Prince of Dragonstone, until Stannis Baratheon seized it during Robert's Rebellion.
castle-sharp-point: Sharp Point is a castle at the northern end of Massey's Hook in the crownlands, seat of House Bar Emmon since its founding by the Andal warlord Togarion Bar Emmon. The house broke from its liege the Storm King to support Aegon Targaryen during the Conquest, and Sharp Point has been sworn to Dragonstone ever since.
castle-stonedance: Stonedance is a castle on the eastern shore of Massey's Hook in the crownlands, seat of the ancient House Massey, said to descend from the First Men. Lord Triston Massey abandoned his liege the Storm King to support Aegon the Conqueror and was rewarded with a seat as the first master of laws on Aegon's small council.
city-oldtown: Oldtown is the oldest and among the largest cities in Westeros, built by the First Men around the Hightower, the seat of House Hightower on Battle Isle. It is home to the Citadel, the seat of the order of maesters, and to the Starry Sept, once the seat of the Faith of the Seven before the High Septon's move to King's Landing after Aegon's Conquest.
city-lannisport: Lannisport is the largest city of the westerlands, grown from a village a mile from Casterly Rock into a walled city with a thriving natural harbor. Ruled by a lesser branch of House Lannister, it prospered from trade and fishing while its parent branch at Casterly Rock rose to become Kings of the Rock and, later, Lords Paramount of the West.
city-gulltown: Gulltown is the largest settlement in the Vale of Arryn, a port city with a sheltered natural harbor at the northern tip of the Bay of Crabs, ruled by House Grafton. Its position makes it a key gateway between King's Landing, Braavos, and the north, and its waters saw a mutually destructive naval battle between House Arryn and House Targaryen during Aegon's Conquest, as well as the opening battle of Robert's Rebellion.
city-white-harbor: White Harbor is the largest city north of the Neck and the North's principal trade port, built at the mouth of the White Knife by House Manderly. The Manderlys were driven from their lands in the Reach roughly a thousand years ago and welcomed into the North as bannermen of House Stark, where they used their southron wealth to found and grow the city around their seat, the New Castle.
castle-harrenhal: Harrenhal is the largest castle in the Seven Kingdoms, raised over forty years on the shore of the Gods Eye by Harren the Black at the cost of thousands of laborers' lives. Aegon the Conqueror burned Harren and his line within its walls using dragonfire, and the ruined, cavernous castle has since gained a reputation for cursing nearly every house to hold it.
castle-riverrun: Riverrun is the ancestral seat of House Tully at the confluence of the Tumblestone and the Red Fork in the western riverlands. First among the river lords to join Aegon the Conqueror against King Harren Hoare, the Tullys were raised to Lords Paramount of the Trident, and the well-defended castle has rarely fallen to siege since.
castle-storms-end: Storm's End is the seat of House Baratheon and one of the strongest castles in the Seven Kingdoms, built at Durran's Point on Shipbreaker Bay by the legendary Durran Godsgrief. It was the ancient seat of the Storm Kings of House Durrandon, and during Robert's Rebellion it withstood a year-long siege under Stannis Baratheon before being resupplied by the smuggler Davos Seaworth.
castle-highgarden: Highgarden is the regional capital of the Reach, a castle of three concentric white stone walls on the Mander held for centuries by House Gardener as the Kings of the Reach. When Aegon the Conqueror slew King Mern IX Gardener at the Field of Fire, the castle's steward Harlan Tyrell surrendered it without a fight and was granted the seat and lordship of the Reach by Aegon.
castle-sunspear: Sunspear, also called the Old Palace, is the seat of House Martell and the capital of Dorne, built on the Broken Arm and surrounded on three sides by the sea. Its towers, raised in Rhoynish fashion after the union of the Martells with the warrior queen Nymeria, were among the few Dornish strongholds the Targaryens never dared burn during their wars of conquest.
castle-the-eyrie: The Eyrie is the seat of House Arryn, a small but famously impregnable castle set high on the Giant's Lance in the Mountains of the Moon. It is known for the Moon Door, an opening in its High Hall through which condemned prisoners are cast to their deaths on the mountainside far below.
castle-winterfell: Winterfell is the ancestral seat of House Stark, said to have been built by Brandon the Builder in the Age of Heroes and ruled by the Starks as Kings of Winter for thousands of years. King Torrhen Stark yielded peacefully to Aegon the Conqueror, after which the Starks continued to rule the North as Lords of Winterfell and Wardens of the North.
castle-pyke: Pyke is the seat of House Greyjoy and the ruling castle of the Iron Islands, its towers and keeps standing on rocky sea stacks connected by swaying rope bridges as the cliff it was built on has eroded away. It has been held by the Greyjoys since Aegon's Conquest, and was heavily damaged during Balon Greyjoy's rebellion against the Iron Throne.
castle-casterly-rock: Casterly Rock is the seat of House Lannister, a stronghold carved into a great stone hill overlooking Lannisport and the Sunset Sea, said to resemble a lion in repose. According to legend it was won from the First Men house of Casterly by the trickster Lann the Clever, and the Lannisters ruled from it as Kings of the Rock until Aegon's Conquest reduced them to Lords Paramount of the West.
castle-greywater-watch: Greywater Watch is the seat of House Reed, rulers of the crannogmen of the Neck in the North. Built upon a moving crannog in the swamps, it is said to never occupy the same spot twice, making it nearly impossible for enemies to find.
castle-oldcastle: Oldcastle is the seat of House Locke on the northern shore of the Bite, southeast of White Harbor. Once the royal seat of the Lockes when they ruled as petty kings after the Long Night, the house was reduced to vassalage by the Kings of Winter and remains sworn to House Stark.
castle-ramsgate: Ramsgate is a minor castle in the North at the mouth of the Broken Branch, between White Harbor and Widow's Watch. It falls within the sphere of influence of House Manderly of White Harbor.
castle-widows-watch: Widow's Watch is the seat of House Flint of Widow's Watch, probably the most powerful of the several Flint branches in the North. Located on a peninsula between the Shivering Sea and the Bite, its founders are said to descend from younger sons of the mountain Flints who left in search of land and wives.
castle-hornwood: Hornwood is the seat of House Hornwood, one of the principal bannermen sworn to House Stark in the North, bearing a brown bull moose as their sigil. Like other northern houses, its lords answered Robb Stark's call to arms during the War of the Five Kings, marching south with the northern host.
castle-the-dreadfort: The Dreadfort is the seat of House Bolton, a grim fortress of thick walls and jagged towers on the Weeping Water. Once the stronghold of the Red Kings who ruled the eastern North before the Starks' ascendancy, it is infamous for House Bolton's ancient practice of flaying enemies and displaying their skins within its halls.
castle-cerwyn: Cerwyn is the seat of House Cerwyn, one of the Starks' closest and most loyal bannermen, located half a day's ride from Winterfell. The house has been tied to House Stark by marriage on several occasions across its history.
castle-deepwood-motte: Deepwood Motte is the seat of House Glover, an old wooden motte-and-bailey castle in the northern wolfswood east of Sea Dragon Point. Once the royal seat of the Glovers when they ruled as petty kings in the Age of Heroes, it was besieged and captured by Asha Greyjoy's ironborn during the War of the Five Kings before being retaken by Stannis Baratheon's forces.
castle-last-hearth: Last Hearth is the seat of House Umber, set in a forest north of Winterfell and the Dreadfort. Its lords led Umber levies south to join Robb Stark's war effort during the War of the Five Kings, leaving the castle in the hands of castellans in their absence.
castle-karhold: Karhold is the seat of House Karstark, founded near the Grey Cliffs after Karlon Stark was granted lands for putting down a rebel lord. As a cadet branch of House Stark and one of its principal bannermen, the Karstarks have intermarried with the Starks of Winterfell repeatedly over the centuries.
castle-flints-finger: Flint's Finger is the seat of House Flint of Flint's Finger, a minor branch of the Flint family in the southwestern North. Once held by the ironborn, it now swears fealty to House Bolton as Wardens of the North.
castle-torrhens-square: Torrhen's Square is the seat of House Tallhart, a stone-walled castle south of the wolfswood and north of Barrowton. Its lords are among the principal houses sworn to House Stark, bearing three green sentinel trees on a brown field.
castle-sows-horn: Sow's Horn is a small stone towerhouse near the border between the crownlands and riverlands, seat of House Hogg, notable for walls unusually thick for its size. It stands close to the lands of House Wode, on ground sworn to House Hayford.
castle-antlers: Antlers is a castle southwest of Maidenpool, near the border between the crownlands and riverlands, seat of the minor noble House Buckwell.
castle-acorn-hall: Acorn Hall is the seat of House Smallwood in the riverlands, a small castle of stone curtain walls and an oak keep southeast of Riverrun. Once sworn directly to House Tully, the Smallwoods fought in several wars across Targaryen history, including the Dance of the Dragons, and are now vassals of House Vance.
castle-pinkmaiden: Pinkmaiden Castle is the seat of House Piper on the Red Fork near the border with the westerlands. Loyal bannermen of House Tully, the Pipers fought on opposing sides of succession crises across Targaryen history, backing Prince Aegon under Maegor I and the Blacks during the Dance of the Dragons.
castle-raventree-hall: Raventree Hall is the seat of House Blackwood in the Blackwood Vale of the riverlands, an ancient castle whose godswood holds a colossal dead weirwood the Blackwoods claim was poisoned by their rivals, House Bracken. The Blackwoods once ruled the riverlands as kings during the Age of Heroes before being driven south into the riverlands by the Kings of Winter.
castle-darry: Darry is the seat of House Darry, once one of the most powerful houses of the Trident before Robert's Rebellion. Steadfast Targaryen loyalists, the Darrys lost much of their standing after backing the losing side of the rebellion, and their lands once stretched as far as the inn at the crossroads.
castle-stone-hedge: Stone Hedge is the seat of House Bracken in the riverlands, an old house whose lords once ruled as river kings during the Age of Heroes. Bitter and ancient rivals of neighboring House Blackwood of Raventree Hall, the Brackens are among the principal bannermen sworn to House Tully of Riverrun.
castle-seagard: Seagard is the seat of House Mallister on the western coast of the riverlands, a fortress built before Aegon's Conquest to guard against ironborn raiders. During Greyjoy's Rebellion, Lord Jason Mallister repelled an ironborn assault beneath its walls, ringing the castle's Booming Tower for only the second time in three hundred years.
castle-the-twins: The Twins are a pair of identical fortified castles belonging to House Frey, joined by a bridge and tower spanning the Green Fork of the Trident. The crossing is one of the most strategically important in Westeros, and the Freys have never failed to collect their toll on it in the six hundred years they have held it.
town-stoney-sept: Stoney Sept is a walled market town in the southern riverlands, built around a fountain shaped like a leaping trout and known for its many bells. It is best remembered as the site of the Battle of the Bells during Robert's Rebellion, where the wounded Robert Baratheon evaded capture by loyalist forces until relieved by Eddard Stark and Hoster Tully.
town-maidenpool: Maidenpool is a walled port town on the Bay of Crabs in the eastern riverlands, seat of House Mooton. Despite being considerably wealthier than their liege lords, House Tully, the Mootons were defeated and brought into the realm during Aegon's Conquest, after the Storm Kings and ironborn kings had ruled the town in earlier centuries.
town-fairmarket: Fairmarket is a market town on the Blue Fork of the Trident in the northern riverlands. It was the site of a decisive battle in which Harwyn Hardhand of the Iron Islands defeated the Storm King Arrec Durrandon to seize control of the riverlands, after which Harwyn's son Halleck Hoare made Fairmarket his seat.
town-barrowton: Barrowton is a town in the North built around the Great Barrow, a great hill topped by Barrow Hall, the seat of House Dustin. Reputed to hold the grave of the First King of the First Men, the barrow gives the barrowlands their name, and the Dustins have ruled as bannermen of House Stark since the last Barrow King submitted at the end of the Thousand Years War.
other-queenscrown: Queenscrown is an abandoned tower holdfast on a small lake island in the New Gift, its name commemorating a visit from Queen Alysanne Targaryen, who stopped there while flying to the Wall on her dragon Silverwing. Villagers painted the tower's merlons gold in her honor, though the settlement has long since been abandoned as the Gift's inhabitants fled south from wildling raids.
castle-castle-black: Castle Black is the headquarters of the Night's Watch and seat of its Lord Commander, standing along the Wall near the northern end of the kingsroad. Unlike a true castle it has no walls of its own beyond the Wall itself, and it is one of only three of the Watch's original nineteen strongholds still garrisoned, alongside the Shadow Tower and Eastwatch-by-the-Sea.
other-moles-town: Mole's Town is a settlement in the Gift a half league south of Castle Black, most of it built underground in warm cellars and tunnels to shelter from the cold. It is best known among the Night's Watch for its brothel, one of the few places brothers of the Watch can find female company.
castle-shadow-tower: The Shadow Tower is the westernmost of the three castles still manned by the Night's Watch, standing where the Wall meets the northern mountains. It guards the western reaches of the Wall with a much smaller garrison than Castle Black, overlooking the abandoned ruins of Sentinel Stand and Westwatch-by-the-Bridge further west.
ruin-westwatch-by-the-bridge: Westwatch-by-the-Bridge is the westernmost of the Night's Watch's abandoned castles, standing near the Bridge of Skulls east of the manned Shadow Tower. Little else is recorded of its history since its abandonment.
ruin-sentinel-stand: Sentinel Stand is an abandoned castle of the Night's Watch along the Wall, lying between the manned Shadow Tower and the ruin of Greyguard.
ruin-greyguard: Greyguard is an abandoned castle of the Night's Watch along the Wall, lying between Sentinel Stand and Stonedoor.
ruin-stonedoor: Stonedoor is an abandoned castle of the Night's Watch along the Wall, lying between Greyguard and Hoarfrost Hill.
ruin-hoarfrost-hill: Hoarfrost Hill is an abandoned castle of the Night's Watch along the Wall, lying between Stonedoor and Icemark.
ruin-icemark: Icemark is an abandoned castle of the Night's Watch along the Wall, lying between Hoarfrost Hill and the Nightfort.
ruin-nightfort: The Nightfort is the oldest and largest of the Night's Watch's castles, twice the age of Castle Black, abandoned during the reign of King Jaehaerys I Targaryen when the Watch could no longer afford to maintain it. Its ruins hold the Black Gate, an ancient weirwood portal through the Wall, and dark legends including the reign of the Night's King and the tale of the Rat Cook.
ruin-deep-lake: Deep Lake is an abandoned castle of the Night's Watch along the Wall, lying between the Nightfort and Queensgate.
ruin-queensgate: Queensgate, once known as Snowgate, is an abandoned castle of the Night's Watch along the Wall, lying between Deep Lake and Castle Black.
ruin-oakenshield: Oakenshield is an abandoned castle of the Night's Watch along the Wall, lying between Castle Black and Woodswatch-by-the-Pool.
ruin-woodswatch-by-the-pool: Woodswatch-by-the-Pool is an abandoned castle of the Night's Watch along the Wall, lying between Oakenshield and Sable Hall.
ruin-sable-hall: Sable Hall is an abandoned castle of the Night's Watch along the Wall, lying between Woodswatch-by-the-Pool and Rimegate. In 50 AC, the knight tasked with restoring it, Ser Raymund Mallery, instead rebelled from the Watch in hopes of claiming the castle as his own seat.
ruin-rimegate: Rimegate is an abandoned castle of the Night's Watch along the Wall, lying between Sable Hall and Long Barrow. In 50 AC, the knight tasked with restoring it, Ser Olyver Bracken, instead rebelled from the Watch in hopes of claiming the castle as his own seat.
ruin-long-barrow: Long Barrow is an abandoned castle of the Night's Watch along the Wall, lying between Rimegate and Torches.
ruin-torches: Torches is an abandoned castle of the Night's Watch along the Wall, lying between Long Barrow and Greenguard.
ruin-greenguard: Greenguard is an abandoned castle of the Night's Watch along the Wall, lying between Torches and Eastwatch-by-the-Sea.
castle-eastwatch-by-the-sea: Eastwatch-by-the-Sea is the easternmost of the three castles still manned by the Night's Watch, a windswept port on the Bay of Seals with the smallest garrison of the three. It serves as the Watch's link to the sea, receiving supplies and reinforcements that the order can no longer sustain across all nineteen of its ancient castles.
other-whitetree: Whitetree is a small free folk village in the haunted forest beyond the Wall, shaded by an immense weirwood tree with a trunk nearly eight feet wide. Its carved face bears signs suggesting the villagers practiced burning their dead within its hollow mouth.
other-crasters-keep: Craster's Keep is a small free folk holdfast in the haunted forest beyond the Wall, home to Craster, a wildling who marries his own daughters and answers to no law but his own. Despite his unsavory reputation, Craster is regarded as a friend to the Night's Watch and has offered its rangers shelter beyond the Wall.
other-fist-of-the-first-men: The Fist of the First Men is a solitary, fist-shaped hill in the haunted forest beyond the Wall, crowned by an ancient ringfort raised by the First Men during the Dawn Age. The Night's Watch made camp within its ruined walls while searching for the missing wildling host during the great ranging beyond the Wall.
